title: Significance of time-convolutionless mode-coupling theory in capturing the dynamics of glass-forming liquids
abstract: This paper demonstrates the significance of the recently proposed time-convolutionless mode-coupling theory (TMCT) in capturing the dynamics of glass-forming liquids. The origin of the primary differences between ideal MCT and TMCT is comprehensively explored from a unified perspective. First, we review two distinct projection operator methods in the Heisenberg picture: the time-convolution (TC) formulation proposed by Mori and the time-convolutionless (TCL) formulation proposed by Tokuyama and Mori. We show that the appropriate choice between these frameworks fundamentally depends on the space-time scales of the relevant variables. In TMCT, the TC formulation is applied to the current density, whereas the TCL formulation is applied to the number density because the latter operates on a significantly longer space-time scale. In contrast, ideal MCT applies the TC formulation to both densities. Consequently, the governing equation in TMCT is timeconvolutionless, whereas the ideal MCT equation features a time-convolution form. This fundamental difference significantly affects various physical quantities near the glass transition. Finally, the full TMCT equation is transformed into a simplified recursion equation to facilitate numerical analysis, and the key predictions of TMCT are compared with those of ideal MCT
summary: This paper rigorously compares Time-Convolutionless Mode-Coupling Theory (TMCT) against standard MCT for glass-forming liquids. It argues that the choice between time-convolution (TC) and time-convolutionless (TCL) formalisms is crucial, showing that TMCT correctly captures the dynamics across different time scales. The results indicate that TMCT yields more physically accurate predictions for the glass transition compared to ideal MCT.
main_problem: The paper aims to demonstrate the superiority of the Time-Convolutionless Mode-Coupling Theory (TMCT) over conventional Mode-Coupling Theory (MCT) in accurately describing the complex dynamics of glass-forming liquids.
main_result: TMCT provides a fundamentally different and more physically consistent description of the dynamics, particularly in the intermediate and long-time regimes, leading to critical parameters that align better with experimental expectations than ideal MCT.
method: The authors compare two projection operator methods (Time-Convolution vs. Time-Convolutionless) and apply them to derive and solve coupled equations for correlation functions, utilizing Laplace transforms and asymptotic analysis.
model_or_system: The system studied is a three-dimensional equilibrium glass-forming liquid, modeled using density-density correlation functions and analyzed across different space-time scales (microscopic, kinetic, hydrodynamic).
key_observables: Intermediate scattering function $f_\alpha(q, t)$, non-Gaussian parameter $\alpha_2(t)$, self-diffusion coefficient $D_L^s$, and the non-ergodicity parameter $f_\alpha(q)$.
important_parameters: Critical volume fraction ($\phi_c$ or $\phi_g$), temperature $T$, and wavevector $q$.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on comparing the full TMCT against ideal MCT, noting that approximations like (A2) lead to unphysical results ($\alpha_2(t) < 0$) in both formalisms.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the classification of basic equations into three distinct space-time scale stages: microscopic [N], kinetic [K], and hydrodynamic [H].
paper_structure: The paper reviews the theoretical basis by comparing TC and TCL projection operators, applies these to the glass-forming liquid dynamics, derives the full TMCT equation, simplifies it into a recursion equation, and finally compares its predictions against ideal MCT across various physical observables and asymptotic limits.
why_interesting: The rigorous comparison of different theoretical frameworks (MCT vs. TMCT) for non-equilibrium dynamics, especially concerning the choice of mathematical formalism (TC vs. TCL), provides deep insights into the limitations of standard theoretical tools when applied to complex many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: (Color online) Classiﬁcation of the basic equations discussed in the present paper into three stages, [N], [K], and [H], depending on a space (r)-time (t) scale, where ℓi and τi indicate the characteristic length and time of interest, respectively. Numbers in the ﬁgure indicate equation numbers.
